title: The Hundred-Year Stone
author: Fablewick
lesson: Trying something new is scary, and you can be scared and still try.
summary: A stone has spent a hundred years sitting in one place. A river is about to change that.
coverIllustration: <svg viewBox='0 0 200 150' xmlns='http://www.w3.org/2000/svg'><path d='M0 100 Q50 95 100 100 T200 105 L200 150 L0 150 Z' fill='#8FA68A' opacity='0.5'/><ellipse cx='100' cy='95' rx='28' ry='20' fill='#5A4E40'/><ellipse cx='95' cy='90' rx='6' ry='4' fill='#FBF6EE' opacity='0.3'/><path d='M30 110 Q60 105 90 112 M120 110 Q150 105 180 112' stroke='#FFFCF6' stroke-width='2' fill='none' opacity='0.6'/></svg>

1.label: Little Listener
1.text:
There was a stone by a small blue river.

The stone had sat there a hundred years. Maybe more.

All day, the stone watched the water. Rush, rush, rush.

“I wonder what it is like to go,” said the stone.

A little silver fish swam up. “Come and see!” said the fish.

“I am scared,” said the stone.

“You can be scared and still come,” said the fish. And off she went. Flick, flick, flick.

The stone thought all day. The stone thought all night.

Then the rain came down. Pit-pat. Pit-pat.

The river grew big. The river grew strong. The water lifted the stone, just a little.

The stone wanted to stay. The stone wanted to go.

The stone let go.

Roll, roll, roll — down the bank, around the bend, all the way to a quiet green pool.

A frog hopped up and sat on the stone’s mossy head.

The stone looked back, far up the river.

“That was scary,” said the stone.

“And I am okay.”

2.label: New Reader
2.text:
There was a stone by the side of a small river. It had sat in that exact spot for a hundred years. Maybe more. Moss grew thick on its head. The stone was a comfortable shape, and it was comfortable being a stone.

Every day, the stone watched the river. The river rushed. The river splashed. The river carried leaves and twigs and quick silver fish away to somewhere else.

The stone had never been anywhere else.

One bright afternoon, a small silver fish stopped beside it.

“You’re always here,” said the fish.

“I am always here,” agreed the stone.

“Do you ever want to come along?” The fish flicked her tail toward the rushing water. “It’s lovely down there. There’s a meadow. There’s a willow tree. There’s a quiet pool with frogs in it.”

The stone was quiet for a long time. Then, very softly, it said, “I think I do want to come. But I am scared. I have always been here.”

The fish thought about that. She was not a very old fish, but she was a kind one.

“You can be scared,” she said, “and still come. Those are not opposite things.”

Then — flick! — she was gone.

The stone thought about it for three whole days. It thought through a sunny morning. It thought through a misty evening. It thought through a long, dark night when the river sounded like a hundred small voices, all saying come, come, come.

On the fourth day, the rain came.

It rained all afternoon. The river grew bigger. The river grew stronger. The water climbed the bank, higher and higher, until it reached the stone, and pushed, gently. Then a little more.

The stone felt the river lifting it. The stone could hold on. The stone could stay.

The stone was very, very scared.

And the stone let go.

The river caught it, soft as a hand, and rolled it, slowly, gently, down the bank, around a bend, past the willow tree, past the meadow, all the way to a quiet green pool where three frogs were arguing about a lily pad.

The stone stopped there. The light fell through the willow leaves in soft green stripes.

The stone looked back, all the way it had come.

“That,” said the stone, mostly to itself, “was very, very scary.”

A frog hopped up and sat on its mossy head, as if the stone had always been there.

“And,” said the stone, “I am okay.”

3.label: On Your Own
3.text:
There was a stone by the side of a small river, and it had been there a long time. Long enough for moss to grow thick over its head. Long enough for a stubborn little fern to set up house in one of its cracks. Long enough that the local mice considered it part of the landscape, like the hill, like the sky.

Nobody could remember a time when the stone had been anywhere else.

The stone watched the river. That was what the stone did. And the river, by the stone’s standards, was a tremendous show. Every day a thousand new things came rushing past: leaves, twigs, the quick silver flash of fish, once a whole duck looking deeply confused about how it had gotten there. The river carried everything away to somewhere else, somewhere downstream, somewhere the stone had never seen.

The stone had been wondering about that somewhere else for a very long time. It had never said so out loud.

Then, one sunlit afternoon, a young silver fish paused beside it, holding herself easily against the current.

“You’re always here,” she said.

“I am always here,” the stone agreed.

“Do you ever want to come along?” She flicked her tail toward the downstream bend. “It’s lovely down there. A meadow full of clover. A willow tree with leaves like long green ribbons. A quiet pool where the river slows and the light turns green and gold. You’d like it.”

The stone was quiet for a long while. The river rushed on.

“I think I do want to come,” the stone said at last, so softly the fish almost missed it. “But I am scared. I have never been anywhere. I don’t know how to be anywhere else.”

The fish considered this. She was not an old fish, but she had been born in a fast current, and fast currents teach you certain things early.

“You can be scared,” she said, “and still come. Those are not opposite things. The scared part doesn’t have to go away first. It can come along too.”

Then she flicked her tail and was gone, downstream, into the bright water.

The stone thought about what she had said for three full days.

It thought through a blue morning, while a kingfisher dove past once, twice, three times. It thought through a foggy evening, when the whole world disappeared and only the sound of the water was left. It thought through a deep, starry night, when the river glimmered like a hundred small voices, and an owl somewhere upstream asked the same question over and over without ever getting an answer.

On the fourth day, the rain came.

It rained all afternoon. It rained into the evening. The river grew. The river darkened. The water climbed higher up the bank than it had in a hundred years, until at last it reached the stone — and lapped at it, and pushed at it, with a soft, patient strength.

The stone could feel the river asking. It could feel itself almost lifting.

And it could feel something else, too: something old and stubborn and frightened. The part of it that wanted, very much, to dig in. To hold on. To wait until the rain stopped and the river went back to being a story it watched from the bank.

The stone was extremely scared.

Then the stone remembered the fish. Those are not opposite things.

The stone let go.

The river took it, soft as a hand, and rolled it gently down the bank. Around the bend. Past the willow with the long green ribbons. Past the meadow that smelled, even in the rain, of sweet clover. Past a place where the river made a small white waterfall, and the stone tumbled — just a little — and was startled, and was, somehow, still all right.

Then the river slowed. And the stone came to rest in a quiet green-gold pool where, just as the fish had promised, three frogs were having an extremely committed argument about a lily pad.

The rain eased. The water settled. The stone lay still, looking up through the willow leaves at a sky already turning pink at the edges.

“That,” the stone said, mostly to itself, “was very, very scary.”

One of the frogs, mid-argument, hopped over and sat on top of the stone, as if the stone had been there forever. A patch of moss began, immediately, to consider its options.

“And,” the stone said softly, “I am okay.”

Upstream, somewhere out of sight, a silver fish flicked her tail once, and was gone.
